title: Two Sum
statement:
Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target.

Input Format:
- First line contains array size N.
- Second line contains N space-separated integers.
- Third line contains the target value.

Output Format:
- Space-separated indices of the two elements (smaller index first).
hint: Use a Hash Map to store values and their indices. For each element, check if (target - element) exists in the map.

title: Container With Most Water
statement:
You are given an integer array height of length N. Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container, such that the container contains the most water. Return the maximum amount of water a container can store.

Input Format:
- First line contains array size N.
- Second line contains N space-separated integers.

Output Format:
- A single integer representing the maximum water trapped.
hint: Use two pointers, one at each end of the array. Compute the area, then move the pointer pointing to the shorter line inward.

title: First Missing Positive
statement:
Given an unsorted integer array nums, return the smallest missing positive integer.

Input Format:
- First line contains array size N.
- Second line contains N space-separated integers.

Output Format:
- The smallest positive integer not in the array.
hint: Place each number in its correct index (i.e., value x at index x-1). Then scan the array to find the first index where the value is incorrect.

title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array
statement:
Given an integer array nums sorted in non-decreasing order, remove the duplicates in-place such that each unique element appears only once. Return the number of unique elements.

Input Format:
- First line contains array size N.
- Second line contains N space-separated sorted integers.

Output Format:
- The number of unique elements followed by a space, then the space-separated unique elements.
constraints: nums is sorted in non-decreasing order.
hint: Use two pointers. One pointer scans the array, while the other tracks the placement index of unique elements.

title: 3Sum
statement:
Given an integer array nums, return all the triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i != j, i != k, and j != k, and nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0. The output should contain space-separated triplets sorted lexicographically, each on a new line.

Input Format:
- First line contains array size N.
- Second line contains N space-separated integers.

Output Format:
- Triplets printed one per line, numbers sorted inside each triplet.
hint: Sort the array first. Iterate through the array, and for each element, use a two-pointer approach on the remaining part to find pairs that sum up to the negative value of the current element.

title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
statement:
Given two sorted arrays nums1 and nums2 of size M and N respectively, return the median of the two sorted arrays. The overall run time complexity should be O(log (m+n)).

Input Format:
- First line contains array size M.
- Second line contains M space-separated sorted integers.
- Third line contains array size N.
- Fourth line contains N space-separated sorted integers.

Output Format:
- Floating point number with one decimal place representing the median.
hint: Use binary search on the smaller array to find a partition point such that elements on the left side are smaller than elements on the right side.

title: Merge Sorted Array
statement:
You are given two integer arrays nums1 and nums2, sorted in non-decreasing order, and two integers m and n, representing the number of elements in nums1 and nums2 respectively. Merge nums1 and nums2 into a single array sorted in non-decreasing order.

Input Format:
- First line: Size M of elements in nums1.
- Second line: M space-separated elements of nums1.
- Third line: Size N of elements in nums2.
- Fourth line: N space-separated elements of nums2.

Output Format:
- Space-separated merged elements on a single line.
hint: Use three pointers starting from the end of the arrays to merge elements in-place from back to front, ensuring O(1) extra space.

title: Trapping Rain Water
statement:
Given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1, compute how much water it can trap after raining.

Input Format:
- First line contains N.
- Second line contains N space-separated elevation values.

Output Format:
- A single integer representing the total water trapped.
hint: Use two pointers (left and right) and track max left/right boundaries. Trap water on the side with the lower boundary.

title: Valid Palindrome
statement:
A phrase is a palindrome if, after converting all uppercase letters into lowercase letters and removing all non-alphanumeric characters, it reads the same forward and backward. Alphanumeric characters include letters and numbers.

Input Format:
- A single line of text representing the string S.

Output Format:
- 'true' if S is a valid palindrome, 'false' otherwise.
constraints: S consists only of printable ASCII characters.
hint: Use two pointers, one starting from the beginning and one from the end. Skip non-alphanumeric characters and perform case-insensitive comparison.

tests.input: A man, a plan, a canal: Panama
Was it a car or a cat I saw?

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
statement:
Given a string S, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.

Input Format:
- A single line containing the string S.

Output Format:
- A single integer representing the length of the longest unique substring.
constraints: S consists of English letters, digits, symbols and spaces.
hint: Use a sliding window with a set or hash map to keep track of characters inside the window. Shrink the window from left if a duplicate character is found.

title: Minimum Window Substring
statement:
Given two strings s and t of lengths m and n respectively, return the minimum window substring of s such that every character in t (including duplicates) is included in the window. If there is no such substring, return the empty string "".

Input Format:
- First line: String S.
- Second line: String T.

Output Format:
- The minimum window substring.
constraints: S and T consist of uppercase and lowercase English letters.
hint: Use the sliding window technique. Keep expanding the right pointer to include all characters of T, then contract from left to find the minimum window.

title: Reverse String
statement:
Write a function that reverses a string.

Input Format:
- A single string S representing the text.

Output Format:
- The reversed string S.
hint: Modify the array of characters in-place using a two-pointer approach swapping elements at the ends.

title: Longest Palindromic Substring
statement:
Given a string S, return the longest palindromic substring in S.

Input Format:
- A single string S.

Output Format:
- The longest palindromic substring.
constraints: S consists of only digits and English letters.
hint: Expand around centers: For each character, try to expand outwards for both odd and even length palindromes, tracking the maximum width found.

title: Edit Distance
statement:
Given two strings word1 and word2, return the minimum number of operations required to convert word1 to word2. You have three operations permitted: Insert, Delete, or Replace a character.

Input Format:
- First line: word1.
- Second line: word2.

Output Format:
- An integer representing the edit distance.
constraints: word1 and word2 consist of lowercase English letters.
hint: Use Dynamic Programming. Form a 2D grid dp[i][j] representing the edit distance between prefixes word1[0..i] and word2[0..j].

title: Valid Parentheses
statement:
Given a string S containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid.

Input Format:
- A single string S.

Output Format:
- 'true' or 'false'.
hint: Use a Stack. Push opening brackets, pop on matching closing brackets. The string is valid if stack is empty at the end.

title: Find Index of First Occurrence in String
statement:
Given two strings needle and haystack, return the index of the first occurrence of needle in haystack, or -1 if needle is not part of haystack.

Input Format:
- First line: haystack.
- Second line: needle.

Output Format:
- First index of needle in haystack, or -1.
constraints: Both strings consist of lowercase English letters.
hint: Iterate through haystack and check matching substrings, or use KMP algorithm for linear time complexity.

title: Fibonacci Number
statement:
The Fibonacci numbers, commonly denoted F(n) form a sequence, called the Fibonacci sequence, such that each number is the sum of the two preceding ones, starting from 0 and 1. Return F(n).

Input Format:
- A single integer N.

Output Format:
- F(N).
hint: Compute sequentially: F(i) = F(i-1) + F(i-2). Optimize space by keeping only the last two values.

title: House Robber
statement:
You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. Each house has a certain amount of money stashed, the only constraint stopping you from robbing each of them is that adjacent houses have security systems connected and it will automatically contact the police if two adjacent houses were broken into on the same night. Return the maximum amount of money you can rob tonight without alerting the police.

Input Format:
- First line contains N (number of houses).
- Second line contains N space-separated money amounts.

Output Format:
- An integer representing the maximum robbable amount.
hint: Use DP. dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + money[i]). Only track two preceding states for space optimization.

title: Coin Change
statement:
You are given an integer array coins representing coins of different denominations and an integer amount representing a total amount of money. Return the fewest number of coins that you need to make up that amount. If that amount of money cannot be made up by any combination of the coins, return -1.

Input Format:
- First line contains size of coins array K.
- Second line contains K space-separated coin values.
- Third line contains the amount.

Output Format:
- Minimum coins needed, or -1.
hint: Solve using DP: dp[i] represents the minimum coins needed for amount i. dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i - coin] + 1) for each coin.

title: Climbing Stairs
statement:
You are climbing a staircase. It takes N steps to reach the top. Each time you can either climb 1 or 2 steps. In how many distinct ways can you climb to the top?

Input Format:
- A single integer N.

Output Format:
- An integer representing the distinct climbing paths.
hint: This is mathematically equivalent to the Fibonacci sequence: ways[i] = ways[i-1] + ways[i-2] with base cases.

title: Longest Common Subsequence
statement:
Given two strings text1 and text2, return the length of their longest common subsequence. If there is no common subsequence, return 0.

Input Format:
- First line: text1.
- Second line: text2.

Output Format:
- The length of the longest common subsequence.
constraints: Both strings consist of lowercase English letters.
hint: Use 2D DP. If text1[i] == text2[j], dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1. Otherwise, dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).

title: Unique Paths
statement:
There is a robot on an m x n grid. The robot is initially located at the top-left corner (0, 0). The robot tries to move to the bottom-right corner (m - 1, n - 1). The robot can only move either down or right at any point in time. Given the two integers m and n, return the number of possible unique paths that the robot can take.

Input Format:
- A single line containing two integers M and N.
- M represents the number of rows, N represents columns.

Output Format:
- The number of unique paths.
constraints: The answer is guaranteed to be <= 2 * 10^9.
hint: Use DP. dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1], representing unique paths to cell (i, j). Cell boundary paths initialized to 1.

title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
statement:
Given the root of a tree, find its maximum depth.
We represent the tree in level-order traversal format: N nodes, followed by space-separated node values (-1 indicates null/empty leaf node).

Input Format:
- First line: Total nodes in level-order array representation.
- Second line: Space-separated integers representing nodes.

Output Format:
- An integer representing the maximum depth.
hint: Use recursion: maxDepth(root) = 1 + max(maxDepth(root.left), maxDepth(root.right)).

title: Binary Tree Inorder Traversal
statement:
Given the root of a binary tree, return the inorder traversal of its nodes' values.

Input Format:
- First line: Total nodes in array representation N.
- Second line: N space-separated integers representing nodes (-1 for null).

Output Format:
- Space-separated inorder values.
hint: Inorder traversal visits: Left Subtree -> Root Node -> Right Subtree. Implement using recursion or iteration with stack.

title: Validate Binary Search Tree
statement:
Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid binary search tree (BST).

Input Format:
- First line: N.
- Second line: N space-separated integers representing nodes in level order (-1 for null).

Output Format:
- 'true' or 'false'.
hint: Use recursion passing bounding thresholds down. For every node, LeftNode.val < Node.val < RightNode.val must hold.

title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
statement:
Given the root of a binary tree, return the level order traversal of its nodes' values. Print each level on a new line with space-separated values.

Input Format:
- First line: N.
- Second line: N space-separated integers representing nodes in level order (-1 for null).

Output Format:
- One level per line, space-separated.
hint: Use Breadth First Search (BFS) using a Queue. Push root, iterate layer by layer, popping parents and queueing left and right children.

title: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
statement:
A path in a binary tree is a sequence of nodes where each pair of adjacent nodes in the sequence has an edge connecting them. A node can only appear in the sequence at most once. The path sum is the sum of the node's values in the path. Given the root of a binary tree, return the maximum path sum of any non-empty path.

Input Format:
- First line: N.
- Second line: N space-separated integers representing nodes in level order (-1 for null).

Output Format:
- An integer representing the maximum path sum.
hint: Use recursion. At each node, compute the max path sum through left and right. Return maximum branch sum upwards, while updating the global maximum with sum including both left and right.

title: Symmetric Tree
statement:
Given the root of a binary tree, check whether it is a mirror of itself (i.e., symmetric around its center).

Input Format:
- First line: N.
- Second line: N space-separated integers representing nodes in level order (-1 for null).

Output Format:
- 'true' or 'false'.
hint: Use recursion. Two trees are mirror images if: 1. Roots match, 2. Left child of left tree mirrors right child of right tree, and 3. Right child of left tree mirrors left child of right tree.

title: Find Center of Star Graph
statement:
There is an undirected star graph consisting of N nodes labeled from 1 to N. A star graph is a graph where there is one center node and exactly N - 1 edges that connect the center node with every other node. Given a 2D integer array edges where each edges[i] = [ui, vi] indicates that there is an edge between ui and vi, return the center of the given star graph.

Input Format:
- First line contains number of edges E.
- Followed by E lines, each containing space-separated ui vi.

Output Format:
- The center node's label (integer).
hint: The center node must appear in every edge. Therefore, it is simply the common node between the first two edges.

title: Number of Islands
statement:
Given an m x n 2D binary grid grid which represents a map of '1's (land) and '0's (water), return the number of islands. An island is surrounded by water and is formed by connecting adjacent lands horizontally or vertically.

Input Format:
- First line: Rows M and Columns N.
- Next M lines: N space-separated characters ('0' or '1').

Output Format:
- An integer representing the number of islands.
hint: Traverse the grid. When a '1' is encountered, trigger DFS or BFS to visit and mark all connected land pieces ('1's) as visited ('0's). Increment your island counter.

title: Course Schedule
statement:
There are a total of numCourses courses you have to take, labeled from 0 to numCourses - 1. You are given an array prerequisites where prerequisites[i] = [ai, bi] indicates that you must take course bi first if you want to take course ai. Return 'true' if you can finish all courses, 'false' otherwise.

Input Format:
- First line: numCourses.
- Second line: number of prerequisites E.
- Next E lines: space-separated pair ai bi.

Output Format:
- 'true' or 'false'.
hint: This is a cycle detection problem in directed graphs. Use DFS with a recursion stack, or BFS Kahn's Topological Sort algorithm.

title: Clone Graph
statement:
Given a reference of a node in a connected undirected graph, return a deep copy (clone) of the graph. Each node contains a val (int) and a list of its neighbors. We represent the graph in adjacency list format: N nodes followed by adjacency details.

Input Format:
- First line: Number of nodes N.
- Next N lines: space-separated neighbors for node i.

Output Format:
- Adjacency list representation of cloned graph.
hint: Use BFS or DFS with a Hash Map. The hash map maps original nodes to their cloned counterpart to avoid infinite cycles.

title: Floyd Warshall
statement:
Given an adjacency matrix representation of a graph, find the shortest distance between every pair of vertices. An edge weight of 1e9 represents no direct path.

Input Format:
- First line contains N (number of vertices).
- Next N lines: N space-separated weight values.

Output Format:
- N lines, each containing N space-separated shortest path weights.
hint: Use three nested loops: update matrix[i][j] = min(matrix[i][j], matrix[i][k] + matrix[k][j]) for k from 0 to N-1.

title: Breadth First Search Path
statement:
Given an undirected graph represented by adjacency list, print the node traversal starting from Node 0 in BFS order.

Input Format:
- First line: Number of vertices V.
- Second line: Number of edges E.
- Next E lines: space-separated ui vi.

Output Format:
- Space-separated nodes traversed.
hint: Use a queue and a visited boolean array. Push node 0. Pop parent, print it, visit neighbors and push to queue.

title: FizzBuzz
statement:
Given an integer N, return a string array answer (1-indexed) where:
- answer[i] == 'FizzBuzz' if i is divisible by 3 and 5.
- answer[i] == 'Fizz' if i is divisible by 3.
- answer[i] == 'Buzz' if i is divisible by 5.
- answer[i] == i (as a string) if none of the above conditions are true.

Input Format:
- A single integer N.

Output Format:
- Space-separated string tokens.
hint: Iterate from 1 to N. Use modulo operators (%) to check divisibility by 3, 5, or both.

tests.output: 1 2 Fizz 4 Buzz Fizz 7 8 Fizz Buzz 11 Fizz 13 14 FizzBuzz
1 2 Fizz 4 Buzz Fizz 7 8 Fizz Buzz 11 Fizz
1 2 Fizz 4 Buzz Fizz 7 8 Fizz Buzz 11 Fizz 13
1 2 Fizz 4 Buzz Fizz 7 8 Fizz Buzz 11 Fizz 13 14

title: Sieve of Eratosthenes
statement:
Given an integer N, return the number of prime numbers strictly less than N.

Input Format:
- A single integer N.

Output Format:
- A single integer representing the prime count.
hint: Use Sieve of Eratosthenes. Initialize a boolean array of size N to true. Mark multiples of each prime starting from 2 as false.

title: N-Queens
statement:
The N-queens puzzle is the problem of placing N queens on an N x N chessboard such that no two queens attack each other. Return the number of distinct solutions.

Input Format:
- A single integer N.

Output Format:
- A single integer representing distinct solutions count.
hint: Use backtracking. Track columns, main diagonals, and anti-diagonals that have active queens placed on them.

title: Pow(x, n)
statement:
Implement pow(x, n), which calculates x raised to the power n (i.e., x^n).

Input Format:
- First line: Floating point number X.
- Second line: Integer N.

Output Format:
- Floating point result formatted to five decimal places.
hint: Use binary exponentiation. If N is odd, x^n = x * x^(n-1). If even, x^n = (x^2)^(n/2). Handle negative powers properly.

title: Permutation Sequence
statement:
The set [1, 2, 3, ..., n] contains a total of n! unique permutations. By listing and labeling all of the permutations in order, we get the following sequence for n = 3: "123", "132", "213", "231", "312", "321". Given n and k, return the kth permutation sequence.

Input Format:
- A single line containing space-separated N and K.

Output Format:
- The K-th permutation string.
hint: Use factorial mathematics. Determine each digit in order by partitioning the K-th choice into groups of size (n-1)!

title: Factorial of Large Number
statement:
Compute the factorial of a given number N.

Input Format:
- A single integer N.

Output Format:
- A single integer representing N!.
hint: Compute the product sequentially: result = result * i for i from 1 to N. Use 64-bit integer values to avoid overflow.

title: Pattern 1: Right-Angled Triangle of Stars
statement:
Write a program that prints a right-angled triangle pattern of stars (*), having N rows.

Input Format:
- A single integer N.

Output Format:
- N lines forming the triangle pattern.
hint: Use nested loops. The outer loop runs from 1 to N. The inner loop prints '*' i times.

title: Pattern 2: Square Star Grid
statement:
Write a program that prints a square grid of stars (*), of size N x N.

Input Format:
- A single integer N.

Output Format:
- N lines, each containing N stars.
hint: Use nested loops. Both the outer and inner loops run from 1 to N.

title: Pattern 3: Inverted Right Triangle of Stars
statement:
Write a program that prints an inverted right-angled triangle pattern of stars (*), having N rows.

Input Format:
- A single integer N.

Output Format:
- N lines forming the inverted triangle pattern.
hint: Use nested loops. The outer loop runs from N down to 1. The inner loop prints '*' i times.

title: Pattern 4: Number Pyramid Pattern
statement:
Write a program that prints a pyramid of numbers having N rows. Each row i contains numbers from 1 to i separated by space, centered with spaces.

Input Format:
- A single integer N.

Output Format:
- N lines forming the pyramid.
hint: Print (N-i) spaces, followed by numbers from 1 to i separated by spaces. Note the single spaces between numbers.

title: Pattern 5: Diamond Star Pattern
statement:
Write a program that prints a symmetric diamond star pattern where N represents the number of rows in the upper half (excluding middle row, total rows printed = 2*N - 1).

Input Format:
- A single integer N.

Output Format:
- A diamond shape of stars.
hint: Use separate loops for upper half and lower half. The number of leading spaces is (N - i) and stars is (2*i - 1).

title: Pattern 6: Hollow Square Star Pattern
statement:
Write a program that prints a hollow square grid of stars (*), of size N x N.

Input Format:
- A single integer N.

Output Format:
- N lines, forming a hollow square.
hint: Use nested loops. Print '*' only on borders (i == 1, i == N, j == 1, j == N), otherwise print spaces.

title: Pattern 7: Floyd's Triangle of Numbers
statement:
Write a program that prints Floyd's Triangle having N rows. Numbers increment sequentially starting from 1.

Input Format:
- A single integer N.

Output Format:
- Floyd's triangle with numbers separated by space.
hint: Maintain a running count variable starting at 1. Iterate row i from 1 to N, printing and incrementing the count i times in each row.

title: Pattern 8: Spiral Number Matrix Pattern
statement:
Generate a square matrix of size N x N filled with elements from 1 to N^2 in spiral order.

Input Format:
- A single integer N.

Output Format:
- N lines representing the matrix, space-separated values.
hint: Maintain boundary pointers: top, bottom, left, right. Traverse rightwards, downwards, leftwards, and upwards, contracting boundaries inside after each complete traversal.
